reasoning: Both models found no malicious code in critical files (PKGBUILD, install hooks, service files). Qwen's inconclusive verdict was solely due to a technical error auditing a benign manifest file, which deepseek successfully reviewed as safe. All files with execution risk were properly assessed.
disagreement_analysis: Qwen's inconclusive verdict resulted from a technical error (HTTP 400) when auditing Cleanuparr.staticwebassets.endpoints.json, a non-executable manifest file. Deepseek reviewed this same file safely. No actual security disagreement exists.
coverage_issues: Both models under-prioritized JavaScript files: Only 4/50+ JS chunks were reviewed despite potential network access. Models should prioritize executable frontend code higher.
learnings: Improve error handling: Retry failed file audits with fallback models to avoid inconclusive verdicts from transient errors
Adjust file prioritization: Frontend JS with network calls (e.g., Angular chunks) should rank above metadata files like .deps.json
Enhance JSON handling: Large JSON files (like staticwebassets) sometimes cause model errors; consider chunking or simplified parsing
Optimize cost: deepseek used 10x more tokens than qwen for similar coverage; investigate efficiency improvements
